definition: The Kalecki-Levy aggregate profit floor from fiscal deficits provides zero protection to firm-level cash flow distress in leveraged credits. A B-rated borrower at 8x true leverage with 70-80% interest expense/EBITDA cannot survive refinancing at 400bp higher regardless of aggregate profit growth.
evidence: Kalecki-Levy identity operates on aggregates, not firm-level
Distressed ratio (HY >1000bp) at ~4-6% is elevated relative to aggregate spread tightness
Empirically confirms quality bifurcation between aggregate benign appearance and firm-level vulnerability
Labeled 'single most valuable original contribution' across both credit analyses in pair_1 debate
open_questions: What share of the leveraged loan universe is vulnerable to refinancing stress?
How does the bifurcation between aggregate health and firm-level distress ultimately resolve?
